macro_segments.title: Two Braisas on Husband vs. Son Inheriting from Property Passing Through the Wife

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Problem of How a Husband Inherits from His Wife
The Two Braisas: Reading Velo Sishov Nachala Through Husband vs. Son
Rashbam's Reading: Both Braisas Present the Same Argument in Different Orders
Tosafos's Reading: Two Different Girsos with Dispute Over Which Pasuk Refers to Husband

macro_segments.title: Locating the Marker for Husband Inheritance: Analysis of 'Ish' and 'Yidbeku'

micro_segments.title: Universal Agreement: The Pasuk with 'Acher' Speaks About the Husband
Attempt to Use 'Ish' as a Signal for Husband, Then Rejection
Attempt to Use 'Veyidbeku' as a Signal for Husband, Then Rejection
Rav Ashi's Resolution: The Word 'Acher' Signals an 'Other' —the Husband
Alternative Signal: 'Matot' (Plural Tribes) vs. 'Mateh' (Single Tribe) Indicates Husband

macro_segments.title: The Husband Does Not Inherit Property ('Ra'uy') That His Wife Was In Line to Receive Before Her Death

micro_segments.title: Question: How Do We Know a Husband Does Not Inherit Ra'uy (Expected but Not Yet Owned Property) the Way He Inherits Muchzak (Already Owned Property)?
Illustration Through Scenarios: When Does Property Pass to Grandson vs. When Does It Pass to Husband?

macro_segments.title: Proof from the Cases of Ya'ir and Pinchas: Son Inherits Ra'uy When Mother Dies Before the Grandfather

micro_segments.title: The Case of Ya'ir: Property He Had That His Father Seguv Did Not Have, Explained Through Mother's Death Before Grandfather's
The Case of Pinchas: Similar Proof That Son Inherits Ra'uy When Mother Dies Before Grandfather
Why Two Cases? Rejecting Alternative Explanations That Property Came Through the Wife or Through Hekdesh

macro_segments.title: The Husband Does Not Inherit Property ('Ra'uy') That His Wife Was In Line to Receive Before Her Death (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tosafos: After the Wife Dies, the Connection to the Husband Severes; Therefore Ra'uy Does Not Go to Him
Distinguishing the Strength of Basar Echad (Marital Union) from Korvah (Biological Relation) in the Hierarchy of Inheritance
Resolution: Husband Inherits from Property Wife Already Possessed; Not from Property Expected but Not Yet Inherited

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Son Before Daughter Applies at All Levels of Inheritance

micro_segments.title: Rule: Sons of Sisters Inherit Before Daughters of Sisters ('Bnei Achot, Lo Bnot Achot')
Midrash Binding Second Inheritance to First: What Applies to Direct Inheritance Applies to Indirect (Through Sister, Uncle, etc.)
Tosafos vs. Rashbam on the Scope of Yerusha Shniya: All Descendants vs. Just the Next Level

macro_segments.title: Inheritance Cases Are Adjudicated Cases ('Din') That Must Begin in Daytime

micro_segments.title: Pasuk: 'V'haya B'yom Hanchilo Et Banav'—Interpreted as Referring to the Adjudication of Inheritance, Not the Timing of Death
Establishing Status: Inheritance Questions Are 'Dinei Mamonos' (Civil Cases) Requiring Beit Din and Beginning in Daytime
Rashbam: The Chidush Is That Inheritance Is a True 'Din' Not Merely Fact-Finding; Tosafos: The Chidush Is That Beit Din Can Impose Judgment
Chukat Mishpat Language Signals Importance: This Is Not Merely a Transaction Governed by Secular Law (Dina D'Malchuta Dina)

macro_segments.title: Exception: If the Court Is Already Occupied with the Case, They May Finish It at Night

micro_segments.title: Rule: Once a Court Begins an Inheritance Case in Daytime and Becomes Occupied ('Asukin') With It, They May Continue and Finish at Night
Source: The Pasuk 'V'Shaftu Et Ha'Am B'Chol Eis'—At All Times—Permits Nighttime Continuation

topical_tags.term: Husband inheritance from wife (baʿal)—textual source and conditions
Bnos Tzelafchad—narrative context and property restrictions
Velo sishov nachala (do not let inheritance pass away)—two psukim, one about husband and one about son
Ra'uy (expected property not yet in possession) vs. Muchzak (already possessed property)
Son inherits from mother's expected property (ra'uy) when she dies before the grandfather
Yair (son of Seguv)—case proving ra'uy passes to grandson, not to father
Pinchas (son of Elazar)—parallel case to Ya'ir for ra'uy inheritance rule
Basar echad (one flesh—marital union) vs. Korvah (biological relation) in inheritance hierarchy
Ben kodem le-bat (son before daughter) at all inheritance levels
Bnei achot (sons of sisters inherit; not daughters of sisters)
Makish yerusha shniya l'yerusha rishona (linking secondary to primary inheritance)
Yerusha rishona and yerusha shniya—scope and application across generations
Chukat mishpat (statute of judgment)—status of inheritance law as Divine enactment, not subject to dina d'malchuta dina
Din vs. fact-finding (birur) in inheritance adjudication
Dinei mamonos (civil cases) require daytime commencement
Beit Din and venue in inheritance disputes
V'haya b'yom hanchilo et banav—interpreted as timing of adjudication, not death
Asukin b'oso inyan (occupied with the case)—exception allowing nighttime continuation
Rashbam—commentary on textual analysis and inheritance law
Tosafos—alternative girsaot and juridical principles in inheritance
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak—explanation of 'yidbeku' (cleaving) as signal for husband
Rav Ashi—explanation of 'acher' (other) as signal distinguishing husband from son
Rabbi Yannai in name of Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha—tradition on husband not inheriting ra'uy
Abaye—challenge to interpretation of 'V'haya b'yom' as timing of death vs. adjudication
Rav Chisda—source permitting nighttime continuation of inheritance adjudication
